story:
“Knock, knock!” A room-service boy steps in, arranges snacks and a bottle of Black Label, and tells me the doorbell is out of order. I think of calling maintenance, then don’t. I’m not expecting anyone important.

I shower, slip into a night-suit, dim the lights, and put on soft instrumental music. I pour a large drink, light a Dunhill with my musical lighter—the one my lost love gifted me—and settle into the hotel’s quiet luxury. Let me introduce myself: M. K. Mehta, from Gujarat, early forties, Regional Sales Manager for The Pioneers, paper-shredders, West India. I travel to Mumbai often. Still single; loved once, it didn’t work out. I feel young enough for wine and women, but I’m tired of solitary evenings. Sometimes I wish the eternal answer would come for me. If death must come, I imagine she would be a woman too.

“Knock, knock!” Two pegs later. At the door is a woman—elegant, confident, exactly my kind of company. “Mr. Mehta?” “That’s me.” The manager knows my tastes and has sent her up. She comes in. The room smells of whisky and smoke; I try a spray of perfume. She laughs it off—no need. I offer a drink. “Beer, if you have it.” I fetch a chilled Haywards 5000, soda, ice. We share a small feast: roasted chicken, snacks, talk. I admire her warmly but keep it respectful; we are two adults choosing the same night. We clink glasses. “Cheers.” “To your beauty,” I say, half-mocking myself. I light her cigarette with my lighter. She calls it beautiful. “A gift from my beloved,” I say. Another round loosens talk into touch, and we move to the bed together.

“Knock, knock!” Startled, I wake. It’s 3:45 a.m. She sleeps peacefully beside me. I tuck the blanket around her, pull on my night-suit, and open the door. A young woman stands there, radiant in white. She is dazzling—so composed she seems not of this world. “Yes? How can I help you?” “I’m afraid you cannot. That’s why I’ve come.” “Who are you?” “You remember me often,” she says gently. “You keep wishing I’d come.” Understanding arrives like light. I’ve been calling her for years without words. “You’re most welcome,” I tell her. “Come in.” “I can’t wait,” she says. “Please. I won’t be long.” She nods, glances toward the bed, and waits in the corridor.

I pour one last drink, swallow it, dress carefully in a business suit, smooth my hair, wear my favorite Lapidus, and take a final cigarette. I stand by the bed and look at the sleeping woman—kind, human, unexpected company. Quietly, I place my wallet there, not as payment, but to ensure she isn’t left uneasy by the morning, and set my lighter beside it, a keepsake to soften any questions. I kiss her softly so she doesn’t wake.

Glass emptied, cigarette stubbed, I survey the room one last time—the dim lights, the silence, the music still whispering. Content at last, I step into the hallway and into the slender, affectionate arms of the long-awaited beloved. Nothing else is disturbed. Not the sleeping woman. Not the new morning’s hush. Only I am gone, as gently as a breath.
